diagnosticQuestions.prompt: A “base” on a chart is roughly:
choices: A period where price mostly tightens or rests before a potential move
A guarantee the stock will double
The same as a bank account

diagnosticQuestions.prompt: A “cup with handle” is best thought of as:
choices: A descriptive nickname for a rounded dip-and-rise with a small pullback
diagnosticQuestions.hint: It’s a shape people recognize — still needs confirmation and risk rules.

teachSlides.title: Why names exist
teachSlides.body: Patterns like cup-with-handle, double bottom, flat base, and ascending base are shorthand for how price rested and tightened. They describe history, not destiny.

teachSlides.title: Breakouts want evidence
teachSlides.body: A breakout means price pushes past a level traders were watching. Serious traders look for volume and context — not one candle alone.

teachSlides.title: Your job
teachSlides.body: Learn to recognize shapes, then always ask: trend, volume, risk, and what you’ll do if wrong.

recapSlide.title: Recap
recapSlide.body: Named patterns summarize how price consolidated. Breakouts can matter — but confirmation, size, and stops matter more than the label.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Pattern names are best treated as:
choices: Useful labels that still need confirmation and risk management
masteryQuestions.explanation: Labels describe shape; edge comes from process.

choices: Two similar lows and a bounce in between
masteryQuestions.explanation: Two tests of a floor area — a classic picture traders discuss.

masteryQuestions.prompt: At a breakout, many traders check volume because:
choices: It can show whether participation supports the move
Volume is always zero on breakouts
Volume replaces the need for a stop
masteryQuestions.explanation: Stronger participation can (not always) validate a push through a level.
